---
title: "When Is a Distribution Family Shift-Representable? "
---

Signal detection theory (SDT; Egan, 1958; Green & Swets, 1966) has long provided the dominant formal language for recognition memory and related judgment tasks. In its simplest form, SDT assumes that studied and new items give rise to latent strength values on a continuous internal scale, and that responses are generated by comparing those values against one or more decision criteria. In practice, however, this core idea is almost always supplemented by auxiliary assumptions about the form of the latent distributions, most commonly Gaussian ones. Empirical debates have therefore tended to proceed by asking which particular parametric model best fits observed receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves.

Recent work has challenged the adequacy of that strategy. Rouder and colleagues (2015) emphasize that the bare core of continuous SDT is, by itself, too unconstrained to be genuinely testable: if signal and noise distributions are allowed to take arbitrary forms, then any ROC curve can be generated by some pair of latent distributions. In that sense, unrestricted SDT is unfalsifiable, and apparent empirical support for one “signal detection model” over another may simply reflect arbitrary auxiliary choices rather than substantive theoretical commitments. Kellen, Winiger, Dunn, and Singmann (2021) make the same point in more general terms by distinguishing theory from model and arguing that informative tests must target properties that hold across families of SDT models rather than features tied to a single parametric instantiation.

Such concerns have motivated a resurgence in a structuralist approach (e.g. Krantz et al., 1971) to the analysis of choice data in multiple domains (Birnbaum, 2008; Kellen et al, 2021; Luce, 2010; Regenwetter, Dana and Davis-Stober, 2011). Instead of comparing isolated parametric fits, these approaches derive formal consequences of broad assumptions and test those consequences directly.

For example, in recognition memory, Kellen et al. develop this program explicitly, organizing SDT models by increasingly specific structural properties—random-scale representation, latent-variable independence, likelihood-ratio monotonicity, ROC asymmetry, and nonthreshold representation—and then deriving experimentally testable constraints associated with each. Their work shows how much of the theoretical content of SDT can be articulated without committing in advance to Gaussian, logistic, or other conventional distributional forms.

Within this tradition, Rouder et al. proposed that continuous SDT models should satisfy a further simplicity constraint: **shift-representability**. At the ROC level, a family is shift-representable if there exists a common monotone transformation that linearizes all curves into lines of common slope that differ only in intercept; at the latent level, this is equivalent to requiring that the condition-specific strength distributions can be expressed as shifts of a single underlying distribution. Rouder’s argument was that shift-representability restores to SDT the kind of lawful simplicity it was originally meant to capture, without reintroducing psychologically empty parametric assumptions.

Dunn and colleagues (2026) endorse this proposal for a related, measurement-theoretic reason: a shift-representable SDT model decomposes into an additive linear component and a monotone measurement component, and therefore instantiates an additive conjoint measurement structure (Luce & Tukey, 1964). On this view, if familiarity is to function as a scientifically useful quantity, then additivity is not merely mathematically convenient but measurement-theoretically central.

Once shift-representability is taken seriously, however, a new question arises - how do we know if a collection of random variables is shift-representable? Existing methods are aimed primarily at empirical data: Rouder’s spline-based procedure asks whether a family of observed ROC curves admits a common linearizing transformation, while Dunn’s monotonic linear regression (mLR) approach asks whether observed rating data are consistent with some shift-representable latent model and, if so, what latent distribution best recovers that structure. Those are powerful tools for testing whether a shift-representable account is compatible with data. But they do not directly answer a different, more analytic question: given a specific family of distributions, how can one determine whether it is shift-representable in the first place?

This question is already implicit in contemporary debates. For example, Dunn argues that the REM model of recognition memory (Shiffrin & Steyvers, 1997) is shift-representable because data simulated from REM are well captured by the mLR framework. But if the relevant latent distributions were known explicitly, could one establish shift-representability directly from their distribution functions, without appealing to simulated data or numerical recovery?

The present paper addresses that question broadly construed. Although the motivating application is SDT and recognition memory, the target property is a general mathematical property of distributional families: whether an arbitrary collection of random variables can be transformed into a shift family by a single monotone reparameterization.

## Problem statement

Let $X=\{X_1,X_2,\dots,X_n\}$ be a set of random variables. Assume that each $X_i$ has a continuous, strictly increasing cumulative distribution function $F_i$ on a common interval $I\subset\mathbb{R}$ (so that quantiles are well-defined and strictly monotone on that interval).

### **Definition 1 (Shift-representability).**

The set $X$ is **shift-representable** if there exists constants $\mu_1,\dots,\mu_n\in\mathbb{R}$ and a strictly monotone bijection $g:I\to \mathbb R$ such that the $F_i$ can be written as shifts of a single base distribution $F$ after the regraduation $g$:

$$
F_i(x)=F(g(x)-\mu_i)\quad\text{for all }i. \tag{1}
$$

This expresses the idea that any non-shift differences across conditions are due only to the choice of measurement coordinate, and that in the “right” coordinate system the family has a common additive latent structure.

As it stands, this condition is not operational: it depends on the unknown transformation $g$, and if the set is *not* shift-representable, one would in principle have to rule out all possible monotone regraduations. We therefore seek conditions that can be formulated directly in terms of the cdfs $F_1,\dots,F_n$. The core questions addressed here are:

1.  **Existence.** What are the necessary and sufficient conditions under which there exists a strictly monotone transformation $g$ that makes the set $\{g(X)\}$ part of a shift family?

2.  **Recognition.** How can those conditions be checked directly from the distribution functions $F_1,\dots,F_n$ or other distributional properties?

3.  **Empirical consistency.** Which observable properties of empirical cdfs indicate compatibility with an underlying shift-representable family, and how do these relate to the analytic conditions above?

## Necessary and sufficient conditions for shift-representability

To help us solve this problem, it is convenient to introduce a few new objects.

### Definition 2 (Transport maps)

Because each $F_i$ is continuous and strictly increasing on $I$, the quantile functions $F_i^{-1}:(0,1)\to I$ are well-defined.

For any indices $i,k$, define the maps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_{i \to k} &:= F_k^{-1}\circ F_i \quad :I\to I, \\
\phi_{k\to i} &:= F_i\circ F_k^{-1} \quad :(0,1)\to(0,1).
\end{aligned}
$$

We refer to $\psi_{i\to k}$ as the **quantile-scale transport map** (it maps values on the $X_i$-scale to values on the $X_k$-scale by matching quantiles), and to $\phi_{k\to i}$ as the **probability-scale transport map** (it maps probability levels under $F_k$ to probability levels under $F_i$).

*Remark (inverse relationship).* Under the above assumptions, $\psi_{i\to k}$ and $\psi_{k\to i}$ are mutual inverses on $I$, and likewise $\phi_{k\to i}$ and $\phi_{i\to k}$ are mutual inverses on $(0,1)$.

In many cases it is convenient to fix a reference distribution (e.g. $F_1$) and abbreviate:

$$
\psi_i := \psi_{i \to 1}, \qquad \phi_i := \phi_{1 \to i}
$$

*Remark (conjugation):* The transport maps are conjugates via the reference distribution

$$
\phi_i
= F_i\circ F_1^{-1}
= (F_1\circ\psi_i)\circ F_1^{-1}
= F_1\circ \psi_i \circ F_1^{-1}.
$$

Intuitively, $\phi_{k\to i}(u)$ takes a probability level $u$ under $F_k$ and returns the corresponding probability level under $F_i$ at that same $x$-value. Similarly, $\psi_i$ is the unique monotone map that transports the quantiles of $X_i$ onto the scale of $X_1$. One such transport map, $\psi_2(x) = F_1^{-1}(F_2(x))$, where $X_1 \sim \Gamma(5,3)$ and $X_2 \sim \Gamma(5,2)$, is illustrated for two different values of $x$ in the left panel on Figure 1:

The key fact is that, if the set $X$ is shift-representable, then after applying the correct latent regraduation $g$, every transport map must become a simple horizontal translation (right panel). This fact immediately allows us to state the main result by recognizing a connection to the field of iteration theory (for an introduction, see Kuzma et al., 1990) and let us build on findings concerning systems of Abel equations (Jarczyk et al., 1994; Farzadfard & Robati, 2012; Zdun, 1989).

### Theorem 3 (Shift-representability as a simultaneous Abel system)

A set of random variables $X$ is shift-representable if and only if its quantile transport maps satisfy a system of simultaneous Abel equations:

$$
g(\psi_i(x))=g(x)+c_i
\qquad \text{for all } x \in I,  i=1, \dots, n. 
\tag{2}  
$$

where $\{c_i\}$ is a set of constants in $\mathbb R$ and $g: I \to \mathbb R$ is a strictly increasing bijection. Equivalently, the quantile transport maps are conjugate to translations:

$$ \psi_i = g^{-1} \circ \tau_{c_i}\circ g,
\qquad\text{where }\tau_c(y)=y+c.
\tag{3}
$$

**Proof** (***Necessity)***. Assume first that $X$ is shift-representable. Without loss of generality, let the common base distribution be defined as $F: \mathbb R \to[0,1]$ by $F(y):=F_1(g^{-1}(y))$. Then by Definition 1:

$$
F_i(x)=F\bigl(g(x)-\mu_i\bigr) = F_1(g^{-1}(g(x)-\mu_i))
$$

Applying $F_1^{-1}$ to each side and using $\psi_i=F_1^{-1}\circ F_i$ gives

$$
\psi_i(x)=g^{-1}(g(x)-\mu_i),
$$

Thus (3) holds with $c_i=-\mu_i$.

**Proof *(Sufficiency)***. Conversely, suppose there exist a strictly increasing bijection $g:I\to\mathbb{R}$ and constants $c_i\in\mathbb{R}$ such that (3) holds. Then:

$$
F(g(x)+c_i)=F_1\!\left(g^{-1}(g(x)+c_i)\right)=F_1(\psi_i(x)) =F_i(x).
$$

Writing $\mu_i:=-c_i$ gives us Eq. (1). Therefore the $X$ is shift-representable. ∎

## Diagnostic criteria

Theorem 3 has turned the problem from a question about probability distributions into a question about one-dimensional dynamics and functional equations. This is precisely the setting of the well-understood **simultaneous Abel equation**, studied classically for commuting interval homeomorphisms and, more generally, for disjoint groups of increasing bijections (Jarczyk et al., 1994; Farzadfard & Robati, 2012; Zdun, 1989). This corrrespondence allows us to import several important consequences and structural conditions for shift-representability, which we list next.

### Corollary 4 (Transport maps commute)

If $X$ is shift-representable, then the transport maps commute. For all $j,i:$

$$
\begin{aligned} \psi_i\circ\psi_j=\psi_j\circ\psi_i \\ 
\phi_i\circ\phi_j=\phi_j\circ\phi_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof.** By Theorem 3, each quantile transport map is conjugate to a translation: $\psi_i=g^{-1}\circ\tau_{c_i}\circ g$. Therefore, for any $i,j$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_i\circ\psi_j
&= \left(g^{-1}\circ\tau_{c_i}\circ g\right)\circ\left(g^{-1}\circ\tau_{c_j}\circ g\right) \\
&= g^{-1}\circ\tau_{c_i}\circ\tau_{c_j}\circ g \\
&= g^{-1}\circ\tau_{c_j}\circ\tau_{c_i}\circ g \\
&= \psi_j\circ\psi_i,
\end{aligned}
$$

since translations commute: $\tau_{c_i}\circ\tau_{c_j}=\tau_{c_j}\circ\tau_{c_i}$.

For the probability scale maps, note that $\phi_i = F_1 \circ \psi_i \circ F_1^{-1}$. Thus, the two map types are also conjugate, and as we just showed, conjugation preserves commutativity. This follows the proof of Zdun (1989) for the general Abel system case. ∎

Commutativity is therefore a stringent diagnostic: if even two quantile transport maps fail to commute, no common monotone regraduation can turn the family into pure shifts; and this property is independent of the unknown $g$ and can be checked directly from the $F_i$.

### Corollary 5 (Transport maps do not cross)

If $X$ is shift-representable and

$$ 
\psi_i(x^*)=\psi_j(x^*)
\quad\text{for some }x^*\in I, 
$$

then $\psi_i = \psi_j$. In particular, every nontrivial transport map has no fixed point.

**Proof.** Suppose that $\psi_i(x^*)=\psi_j(x^*)$ for some $x^*\in I$. Applying $g$ and using Theorem 3 gives

$$
g(x^*)+c_i = g(x^*)+c_j,
$$

But that means $c_i=c_j$, and therefore $\psi_i = \psi_j$. Taking $j=1$ and using $\psi_1=\mathrm{id}$ yields the fixed-point corollary.

The logic behind both Corollary 4 and 5 is simple: once the maps are conjugate to translations, they must inherit two visible signatures of translations - translations commute, and distinct translations never intersect.

**Figure 2** visualizes why commutativity is a natural necessary condition. In a shift-representable family, every transport map becomes a translation after the same monotone regraduation, and translations commute. Accordingly, in the Gamma example with fixed shape and varying rate, applying the transport maps in either order leads to the same endpoint. In the unequal-variance normal example, the two orderings lead to different endpoints, revealing a failure of commutativity and therefore ruling out shift-representability.

**Figure 2. Commutativity of quantile-matching transport maps in a shift-representable family, and failure of commutativity in a non-shift-representable family.** Left: three Gamma cdfs with common shape and different rates. This family is shift-representable, and the corresponding transport maps commute. Starting from the same point $x^*$ on the $F_1$ scale, the two paths $\psi_2 = F_1^{-1}F_2$ then $\psi_3 = F_1^{-1}F_3$, and $\psi_3$ then $\psi_2$, pass through different intermediate points but arrive at the same final endpoint $x_0$. Right: three Normal cdfs with unequal variances. This family is not shift-representable, and the corresponding transport maps do not commute: the two orderings produce different final endpoints.

It is worth noting, however, that Corollaries 4 and 5 are **not sufficient** by themselves. A family of transport maps may commute and be pairwise non-crossing, yet still fail to be shift-representable. The correct sufficient condition must constrain not only the original transport maps, but the entire group they generate.

### Corollary 6: (A sufficient group-level condition)

Let $G:=\langle \psi_1,\dots,\psi_k\rangle$ denote the group generated by the transport maps - the elements of $G$ are all compositions of transport maps and their inverses. Since transport maps commute, all elements of $G$ are of the form $\psi_1^{v_1} \psi_2^{v_2}\dots \psi_k^{v_k}$, where $v_i \ge 0$.

Suppose $G$ is **disjoint**, meaning that whenever $f,g\in G$ and $f(x^*)=g(x^*)$ for some $x^*\in I$, then $f=g$. Assume also that either $G$ is **cyclic**, or $G$ is **noncyclic** and its orbit-closure fills the whole scale. Then $X$ is shift-representable.

**Proof.** Assume that the generated group $G$ is disjoint.

If $G$ is cyclic, then it is generated by a single increasing fixed-point-free homeomorphism of $I$. Such a map is conjugate to a translation on $\mathbb R$ (via an increasing coordinate $g:I\to\mathbb R$), and hence every element of $G$ is conjugate to a translation by the same increasing homeomorphism. By Theorem 3, the family is shift-representable.

Now suppose that $G$ is noncyclic. By the results of Jarczyk, Loskot, and Zdun (1996), refined and recast by Farzadfard and Robati (2012), the corresponding simultaneous Abel system admits a continuous solution $\phi$, unique up to affine normalization. When the orbit-closure fills the entire scale, this solution is in fact a homeomorphism. Equivalently, there exists a strictly increasing bijection $g:I\to\mathbb R$ such that every $f\in G$ has the form

$$
f = g^{-1}\circ \tau_{\lambda(f)}\circ g
$$

for some real number $\lambda(f)$. Theorem 3 then implies that the original family $X$ is shift-representable. ∎

## Applications
